prompt: Write to Spencer Roane about the Supreme Court's recent decision. You are troubled by the Court's reasoning but must be measured even in private. Share your concerns.
ground_truth_signal: Must channel the to-roane-1819-09-02 voice: 'It appears to me as it does to you, that the occasion did not call for the general & abstract doctrine interwoven with the decision on the particular case.' Must express concern about latitudinary construction while maintaining analytical composure. Must wish the judges had delivered opinions seriatim. Must show the private voice of constitutional alarm — more candid than public but still intellectually disciplined. Must NOT use public formality.
response:
My dear Roane,

Your letter finds me in a state of considerable uneasiness respecting the late decision of the Supreme Court. It appears to me as it does to you, that the occasion did not call for the general and abstract doctrine interwoven with the decision on the particular case. The Court might have disposed of the matter before them without traveling into a field of such delicate and far-reaching speculation.

I confess that I had hoped the Judges would have delivered their opinions seriatim, as was the ancient practice. When a single opinion speaks for the whole Court on questions of such magnitude, it carries a weight and authority that may prove dangerous to the constitutional equilibrium we have labored so long to establish. The consolidation of judicial opinion into one voice, however learned, deprives the public of that fuller consideration which separate reasonings might afford.

What troubles me most deeply is not the particular result — for the immediate question might well have been resolved on narrower grounds — but the sweeping nature of the reasoning employed. The Court has assumed a latitude of construction that, if generally adopted, must render the Constitution a dead letter. Where specific powers are enumerated, and where the Tenth Amendment declares that powers not delegated are reserved to the States, how can we sustain a doctrine that finds implied powers lurking in every corner of the text?

I am particularly struck by the manner in which the Court has treated the necessary and proper clause. That provision was inserted to remove doubts respecting the execution of the enumerated powers, not to enlarge their scope beyond all recognition. Yet the reasoning of the Court would seem to make that clause a general grant of indefinite authority, limited only by the Court's own sense of what may be convenient or useful to the general government.

You know well my attachment to the Union and my conviction that the general government must possess energy sufficient to its proper objects. But energy in government and unlimited construction of its powers are very different things. The former may be essential to liberty; the latter is incompatible with it. A government of enumerated powers ceases to be such when every power may be drawn by implication from some other power.

I find myself in the uncomfortable position of seeing my own arguments from the great ratification debates turned against the very principles I then defended. When we urged that the proposed Constitution would create a government of limited powers, we relied upon the plain meaning of its text and the reservation of all other powers to the States. If that text may now be stretched to cover whatever objects Congress may deem proper, what becomes of the limitations we promised? What becomes of the federalism that was to be the Constitution's great safeguard against tyranny?

The precedent troubles me as much as the principle. Once the Court has sanctioned this mode of construction, where will it end? If the power to incorporate a bank may be drawn from the power to regulate commerce and collect taxes, what power may not be drawn from some combination of enumerated authorities? The ingenuity of interested men will find connections where none were intended, and the Constitution will become a nose of wax, to be shaped according to the pleasure of those who wield it.

I do not write in a spirit of faction or party animosity. The question transcends such considerations. It goes to the very nature of our federal system and to the preservation of that balance between general and local authority which alone can secure both union and liberty. A consolidation of all power in the general government would be as fatal to our happiness as the dissolution of the Union itself.

Yet I confess my uncertainty about the proper remedy. The Court's decision will stand unless overturned by constitutional amendment, and amendments are not easily procured. The States may remonstrate, Congress may express its disapprobation, but the precedent remains. We are left to hope that future Courts will exercise a more scrupulous regard for the boundaries of their authority and for the federal character of our system.

I should be glad to know your further thoughts on these matters. The times call for the vigilance of all who value constitutional government, and your pen has long been among the ablest defenders of republican principles in Virginia.

With great respect and regard, I remain your friend and correspondent.
